title: 6.C35 A2: Exploratory Data Analysis
image: images/A2/Avg Corp Own Rate vs. Race Distribution.png
description: Exploratory data analysis of Boston housing ownership (2004 to 2024) that visualizes how corporate-owned and owner-occupied rates shift over time and across neighborhoods.
story: This project began as an open-ended exploration of how housing ownership patterns vary across Boston. By iteratively asking questions and building visualizations, I traced differences in corporate ownership, owner occupancy, and neighborhood-level change over time. The process helped me move from broad curiosity to more focused insights about how geography and demographics shape housing trends.

title: 6.C35 A3: Visualization Critique & Redesign
description: Critiqued and redesigned a COVID-19 data visualization, using iterative sketching and visual analysis to improve interpretability,temporal comparison, and communication of magnitude.
story: I started by analyzing how the original visualization communicated seasonality and change over time, then identified places where the design made comparison and magnitude harder to read. Through critique and sketching, I developed a redesign that preserved the core idea while making the chart more legible and interpretable. The project showed me how even small visual decisions can strongly affect what patterns viewers notice first.

title: 6.C35 A4: Persuasive or Deceptive Visualization?
description: Designed contrasting visualizations from the same dataset to examine how distinct design choices can support different narratives while raising questions about visualization ethics.
story: Using the same dataset, I created two visualizations that pushed viewers toward very different conclusions through changes in framing, emphasis, and design choices. The goal was to show how visualizations do not just present information, but also shape interpretation. This project made the ethical dimension of data storytelling much more concrete by demonstrating how easily design can persuade, or mislead.

title: Moonlight Queue Optimizer
description: Modeling the fastest path through an imaginary late-night bakery with unreasonable demand for croissants.
story: The problem began with a line out the door and a cashier moving at the speed of philosophical reflection. I started sketching customer flow patterns on napkins and discovered that most inefficiency came from people deciding what they wanted only when it was too late. The experiment became a study in bottlenecks, patience, and the strange emotional intensity of pastry logistics. The conclusion was simple: systems fail when they assume humans arrive prepared.

title: Pigeon Transit Authority
description: Designing a speculative public transportation network optimized for pigeons instead of people.
story: Cities already belong to pigeons in every practical sense, but their infrastructure remains embarrassingly underdeveloped. I imagined a transit system with seed-based incentives, roof-to-roof express routes, and weather-sensitive landing zones. What started as a joke turned into a surprisingly serious exercise in thinking about mobility from a nonhuman perspective. It made one point very clearly: every network reveals who it was built for, and who it quietly ignores.

title: Museum of Misplaced Keys
description: Cataloging fictional lost keys to build an archive of tiny everyday mysteries.
story: A lost key is a very small object with a disproportionate emotional radius. Each missing key suggests a locked door, a missed routine, or a moment of mild domestic chaos. The project imagined an archive where every unlabeled key came with a speculative history, somewhere between detective work and storytelling. In the process, it became less about keys and more about the invisible systems of trust and access that structure ordinary life.

title: Weather Forecasts for Indoor Moods
description: Creating a mock meteorological system that predicts the emotional climate of rooms rather than the outdoors.
story: Some rooms are sunny even under fluorescent lights, while others feel overcast no matter how many windows they have. I built a playful framework for forecasting tension, focus, comfort, and collective awkwardness using the language of weather reports. The premise was absurd, but the observations felt uncomfortably real. It ended up being a way to think about atmosphere as a design problem, not just an accidental byproduct of architecture.

title: The Department of Unsent Postcards
description: Imagining a bureaucracy dedicated to processing postcards that were bought with good intentions and never mailed.
story: Every postcard begins as a promise: I was here, I thought of you, I meant to send proof. Most of them never make it past that stage. This project treated unsent postcards as a category of unfinished communication and built a fictional office to sort them by excuse, destination, and emotional delay. Beneath the humor was a quieter question about how often affection fails for logistical reasons instead of emotional ones.

title: Cloud Naming Convention Committee
description: Proposing an overly formal taxonomy for clouds based on personality, attitude, and dramatic timing.
story: Traditional cloud categories are scientifically useful but emotionally incomplete. Some clouds hover with purpose, some drift like they have nowhere important to be, and some clearly arrive just to make a point. I invented a naming system that classified them less by altitude and more by temperament. The joke carried farther than expected, becoming a reflection on how humans always invent labels that reveal as much about ourselves as the things we classify.

title: Escalator Etiquette Simulator
description: Simulating the fragile social order of standing, walking, and hesitating on escalators.
story: Escalators look mechanical, but their real complexity is social. Everyone arrives with a private theory about where to stand, how fast to move, and whether pausing at the top is acceptable behavior. I built a speculative simulator around these tiny collisions of expectation. The result was a study in unspoken rules, public choreography, and how quickly civilization can weaken when one person forgets how spatial courtesy works.

title: Archive of Future Nostalgia
description: Collecting imagined memories of things that have not happened yet, but already feel worth missing.
story: Most archives document what has been, but this one tried to preserve what people suspect they will someday miss. It included future train rides, conversations not yet over, and places still ordinary enough to be overlooked. The project sat somewhere between design fiction and emotional forecasting. Its central idea was that nostalgia is often less about the past than about recognizing, too late, what mattered while it was still present.

title: Silent Disco for Houseplants
description: Inventing a soundless performance system for plants that deserve better ambiance than passive sunlight.
story: Houseplants are expected to perform photosynthesis with minimal emotional support. I found this rude. So I imagined a silent disco format using light pulses, timed shadows, and humidity shifts as a kind of botanical entertainment. The project was unserious in premise but sincere in curiosity, asking what care looks like when it becomes imaginative rather than purely functional. At minimum, it improved the dignity of ferns.
